title: MLX: Apple's Native ML Framework
description: MLX is Apple's NumPy-flavoured array framework built natively on unified memory with lazy evaluation. Learn when MLX beats PyTorch+MPS, how mx.eval() works, and the mlx-lm ecosystem for running Llama and Mistral on a Mac.

sections.title: What MLX Is and Why It Exists
sections.content:
### A second array framework, on purpose

Apple shipped **MLX** in late 2023 as a research-grade array framework designed *from scratch* for Apple Silicon. It is not a PyTorch competitor in the traditional sense — it's a more focused tool with a few sharp opinions:

1. **Unified memory is a first-class assumption.** There is no `.to(device)`. Arrays just live in RAM, and CPU/GPU/ANE can read the same buffer with no copy.
2. **Lazy evaluation by default.** Operations build up a small graph; nothing actually runs until you `mx.eval()` (or implicitly cause materialization by `.item()`, printing, or numpy interop).
3. **NumPy-shaped API.** Most code that already runs on NumPy translates with a one-line import change.
4. **Composable function transforms** — `mx.grad`, `mx.vmap`, `mx.compile` — borrowed from JAX.
5. **Apple-only.** No Linux, no Windows, no NVIDIA. The narrowed scope is the point: every line of MLX assumes M-series hardware.

In practice, MLX exists because PyTorch's MPS backend has fundamental limits: it inherits PyTorch's CUDA-shaped abstractions (separate device tensors, cudaMemcpy-style transfers, eager-by-default execution), and those abstractions don't map cleanly onto a chip with one shared memory pool. MLX is what you get if you redesign the framework knowing the hardware.

### The library at a glance

```python
import mlx.core as mx          # the array library (NumPy analogue)
import mlx.nn as nn             # neural-network modules (PyTorch analogue)
import mlx.optimizers as optim  # optimizers
import mlx.utils as utils       # tree_map, tree_unflatten
```

A tiny example:

```python
import mlx.core as mx

a = mx.random.normal((1024, 1024))
b = mx.random.normal((1024, 1024))
c = a @ b + 1.0
print(c.shape)        # (1024, 1024)
# Note: nothing has actually computed yet.

mx.eval(c)            # NOW the matmul + add runs on the GPU
```

### Where MLX wins, today

Fair comparisons on M-series hardware:

- **LLM inference (mlx-lm with quantized weights):** MLX is currently the fastest path on Mac. 4-bit Llama-3 8B runs at ~50–70 tok/s on M2 Max, ~15–25% faster than PyTorch+MPS at the same precision, and with much lower memory.
- **Small-to-medium training:** MLX is comparable to PyTorch+MPS on common transformer architectures, sometimes 10–20% faster, sometimes slower. It depends on op coverage and which kernels MLX has tuned.
- **Research code with custom transforms:** if you reach for `vmap` or want to write functional-style code, MLX feels much closer to JAX and is more pleasant than PyTorch.

Where MLX loses:

- **Ecosystem.** Hugging Face Transformers, Datasets, Accelerate, Lightning — all PyTorch-first.
- **Distributed training.** PyTorch+MPS doesn't really do this either, but MLX's distributed story is even thinner.
- **Production inference servers.** Most existing infra wants a PyTorch or ONNX checkpoint.

> **Quick check.** Why does MLX *not* have a `.to(device)` API? *(Because under unified memory there's no device transfer to do — every array is reachable by both the CPU and the GPU directly.)*

sections.title: Lazy Evaluation in Detail
sections.content:
### What "lazy" actually means here

In PyTorch (eager mode), `a + b` runs the addition immediately and gives you a tensor with concrete data. In MLX, `a + b` returns an array whose **value is unknown** — what's stored is a small node in a computation graph saying "add the values of `a` and `b` when someone asks".

```python
import mlx.core as mx

a = mx.array([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
b = mx.array([4.0, 5.0, 6.0])
c = a + b              # graph node, no compute yet
d = c * 2 + a          # more graph, still no compute

print(d)               # implicit eval: prints [6, 9, 12]
```

Four things force materialization:

1. **`mx.eval(*arrays)`** — explicit, evaluates the listed arrays.
2. **`.item()`** — pulls a single Python scalar; obviously needs the value.
3. **`np.array(x)` or `x.tolist()`** — interop boundaries.
4. **`print(x)` / repr** — under the hood calls `mx.eval` first.

Nothing else triggers a run. This includes other ops on the same array — those just add more graph nodes.

### Why this matters for performance

Lazy evaluation lets MLX do two things eagerly is bad at:

- **Operator fusion across many statements.** If you write `(a * b + c).sum()` as three steps, MLX can still fuse them into one kernel because it sees the whole expression at `eval` time. Eager PyTorch would launch three kernels.
- **Dead-code elimination.** If you build a tensor and never `eval` (or never use) it, MLX simply doesn't compute it.

The practical advice: write naturally, don't `eval` after every line, and let MLX schedule larger chunks. **Do** call `mx.eval` at the boundaries of work — end of a training step, before logging, before saving.

### Lazy vs. PyTorch eager + `torch.compile`

This is the right comparison; both close the gap on operator fusion but in different ways.

| Aspect | MLX (lazy) | PyTorch eager | PyTorch + `torch.compile` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Default mode | lazy graph | eager | traced + compiled |
| Fusion across statements | yes, automatic | no | yes, after warm-up |
| First-call cost | small per-shape compile | none | large compile |
| Debuggability | mid (graph hides errors until eval) | best | rough; recompiles confuse stack traces |
| Dynamic shapes | clean | clean | painful (graph breaks) |

MLX's model sits between eager PyTorch and JIT-compiled PyTorch: cheaper than `torch.compile` to warm up, more fusion than eager.

### Benchmarking pitfalls

This is the most common MLX tripwire:

```python
import time, mlx.core as mx

a = mx.random.normal((4096, 4096))
b = mx.random.normal((4096, 4096))

t0 = time.perf_counter()
c = a @ b              # builds graph; ~zero time
print(f"{(time.perf_counter()-t0)*1e3:.3f} ms")  # microseconds, suspiciously

t0 = time.perf_counter()
mx.eval(c)
print(f"{(time.perf_counter()-t0)*1e3:.3f} ms")  # the real number
```

Always wrap the timed region with `mx.eval(...)` at the end. The same hazard applies to PyTorch (where you need `torch.mps.synchronize()`), and to JAX (`block_until_ready`).

### Function transforms

MLX provides composable function transforms inspired by JAX:

```python
def loss_fn(params, x, y):
    pred = model.apply(params, x)
    return ((pred - y) ** 2).mean()

grad_fn = mx.grad(loss_fn)              # returns a fn computing grads w.r.t. params
batched = mx.vmap(loss_fn, in_axes=(None, 0, 0))   # auto-batching over axis 0
fast = mx.compile(loss_fn)              # graph compile + cache
```

You can chain them: `mx.compile(mx.grad(loss_fn))` is the canonical fast-training-step pattern.

> **Gotcha.** Don't `mx.eval` inside an inner loop if you can help it. Calling eval after every statement throws away most of the lazy-eval benefit; a typical training step `eval`s once per step (on the loss + the updated parameter pytree).

sections.title: Unified Memory: The Real Power Move
sections.content:
### One pool of RAM, multiple readers

On an M-series chip, the GPU and CPU share the same physical RAM through a single memory controller. MLX exploits this directly: arrays are not "on the GPU" or "on the CPU" — they're in RAM, and any compute unit can address them.

Concrete consequences:

- **No `.to(device)`.** Arrays don't have a device attribute in the PyTorch sense. There's a `default_device` that controls *which compute unit runs the next op*, but no copy happens when you switch.
- **Zero-copy NumPy interop.** `np.array(x)` shares memory with the MLX array (after eval). For small post-processing you don't pay a transfer.
- **Larger models fit.** On a 64 GB Mac you can load a 60 GB model and *use* it from the GPU, where on a discrete-GPU system you'd be limited to whatever VRAM you bought (often 24 GB).
- **Cheap inspection.** `print(x)` and debugging are essentially free; no GPU→CPU copy in the human-perceptible sense.

### Where unified memory matters most: large models

The practical M-series memory ceilings, as of M3/M4:

| Mac config | Total RAM | Largest model that fits comfortably (with KV cache, fp16) |
|---|---|---|
| MacBook Air, 16 GB | 16 GB | ~7B fp16 or ~13B 4-bit |
| MacBook Pro, 36 GB | 36 GB | ~30B fp16 or ~70B 4-bit |
| MacBook Pro / Studio, 64 GB | 64 GB | ~70B fp16 or ~120B 4-bit |
| Mac Studio, 192 GB | 192 GB | 405B 4-bit feasible |

These are inference numbers; training is more memory-hungry due to optimizer state and activations.

### Setting the default compute unit

```python
import mlx.core as mx

mx.set_default_device(mx.gpu)   # default; runs ops on the GPU
mx.set_default_device(mx.cpu)   # forces ops onto the CPU (debugging, fallback)
```

You can also put a single op on a specific device with `mx.eval(x, stream=mx.cpu)` and similar; the public API is small and stable.

### Memory pressure on macOS

Unified memory doesn't free you from the physical limit. If you allocate more than the chip has, macOS swaps to SSD — fast, but at least 30x slower than RAM. Practical advice:

- Watch *Activity Monitor → Memory*. "Memory pressure" green is fine, yellow means you're swapping, red means stop.
- For LLM inference, leave at least 4 GB headroom for the OS and other apps. A 64 GB Mac shouldn't be loading a model that wants 62 GB.
- MLX has aggressive caching of intermediate buffers. Call `mx.metal.clear_cache()` between unrelated workloads if you're tight on memory.

> **Gotcha.** Even though unified memory eliminates copies, it doesn't eliminate cache misses. Hot-path tensors should still be sized to fit in the GPU's L2 cache when possible — bandwidth-bound kernels still benefit from data locality.

sections.title: Building and Training in MLX
sections.content:
### `mlx.nn` mirrors PyTorch's `torch.nn`

If you know PyTorch, the API will feel immediately familiar:

```python
import mlx.core as mx
import mlx.nn as nn

class SmallMLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_dim, hidden, out_dim):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(in_dim, hidden)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(hidden, out_dim)

    def __call__(self, x):                  # MLX uses __call__, not forward
        return self.fc2(nn.relu(self.fc1(x)))

model = SmallMLP(784, 256, 10)
mx.eval(model.parameters())                  # materialize lazily-initialized weights
```

Key differences from PyTorch to keep straight:

- **Forward pass is `__call__`**, not `forward`.
- **No `.to(device)`** anywhere; the model already lives in unified memory.
- **No `requires_grad`.** Gradients are obtained functionally via `mx.grad` / `mx.value_and_grad`.
- **Parameters are returned as a nested dict** (a "pytree"), not a flat list of `nn.Parameter` objects.

### A complete training loop

```python
import mlx.core as mx
import mlx.nn as nn
import mlx.optimizers as optim

model = SmallMLP(784, 256, 10)
optimizer = optim.AdamW(learning_rate=1e-3)

def loss_fn(model, x, y):
    logits = model(x)
    return nn.losses.cross_entropy(logits, y, reduction='mean')

loss_and_grad_fn = nn.value_and_grad(model, loss_fn)

for x, y in train_loader:                      # any iterable yielding mx.arrays
    loss, grads = loss_and_grad_fn(model, x, y)
    optimizer.update(model, grads)
    mx.eval(model.parameters(), optimizer.state, loss)   # materialize the step
```

Notice the explicit `mx.eval` at the end of each step. That's the natural place to materialize: parameters and optimizer state need to be concrete before the next iteration's graph is built, and `loss` needs to be concrete before you log it. *Don't* eval inside the gradient computation.

### Parameter / state mapping

A quick PyTorch-to-MLX cheat sheet for the parts you'll hit first:

| PyTorch | MLX |
|---|---|
| `torch.tensor([1, 2])` | `mx.array([1, 2])` |
| `x.to('mps')` | (nothing; arrays already in unified memory) |
| `model.forward(x)` | `model(x)` (`__call__`) |
| `loss.backward()` | grads come from `mx.grad` / `nn.value_and_grad` |
| `optimizer.step()` | `optimizer.update(model, grads)` |
| `model.state_dict()` | `dict(tree_flatten(model.parameters()))` |
| `torch.save(state, p)` | `mx.save_safetensors(p, params)` |
| `torch.load(p)` | `mx.load(p)` (returns dict) |
| `model.eval()` / `model.train()` | `model.eval()` / `model.train()` (same names) |
| `with torch.no_grad()` | (no grads unless you ask via `grad`) |

### Speeding up the step

The canonical fast pattern uses `mx.compile`:

```python
@mx.compile
def step(model, optimizer, x, y):
    loss, grads = loss_and_grad_fn(model, x, y)
    optimizer.update(model, grads)
    return loss

for x, y in train_loader:
    loss = step(model, optimizer, x, y)
    mx.eval(model.parameters(), optimizer.state, loss)
```

`mx.compile` traces the function once per shape signature, fuses ops aggressively, and caches the compiled kernel. First call is slower, subsequent calls are faster. This is MLX's analogue of `torch.compile` and tends to yield 10–25% speedups on transformer training loops.

sections.title: mlx-lm: Running Open-Weight LLMs Locally
sections.content:
### A focused tool for one job

`mlx-lm` is a separate package layered on top of MLX that does one thing well: load and run open-weight transformer LLMs on Apple Silicon. It speaks the Hugging Face checkpoint format, supports 4/8-bit quantization, and gives you both a CLI and a Python API.

Install:

```bash
pip install mlx mlx-lm
```

### Generate from the command line

```bash
mlx_lm.generate \
    --model mlx-community/Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct-4bit \
    --prompt "Explain unified memory in one paragraph." \
    --max-tokens 200 \
    --temp 0.7
```

The `mlx-community` org on Hugging Face hosts pre-quantized MLX-format weights for Llama 3, Mistral, Qwen, DeepSeek, Phi, Gemma, and dozens of others. Pick a `*-4bit` or `*-8bit` repo for inference; pick the full-precision repo if you plan to fine-tune.

### Quantize a Hugging Face checkpoint yourself

```bash
mlx_lm.convert \
    --hf-path mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 \
    --mlx-path ./Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3-4bit \
    -q --q-bits 4 --q-group-size 64
```

The quantization is GPTQ-style block quantization with a per-group scale and zero point. q-group-size is the only knob worth tuning: 32 gives slightly better quality, 64 is the default, 128 saves a bit of memory at a small accuracy cost.

### Generate from Python

```python
from mlx_lm import load, generate

model, tokenizer = load("mlx-community/Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct-4bit")

prompt = tokenizer.apply_chat_template(
    [{"role": "user", "content": "What's the difference between MPS and MLX?"}],
    tokenize=False, add_generation_prompt=True,
)

out = generate(model, tokenizer, prompt=prompt, max_tokens=200, verbose=True)
print(out)
```

`verbose=True` prints tokens-per-second when generation finishes — handy for benchmarking.

### Realistic numbers

On an M2 Max (38-core GPU, 64 GB):

| Model | Quant | First-token | Steady tok/s | Peak RAM |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Llama 3.2 3B Instruct | 4-bit | ~0.3 s | 80–110 | 2.5 GB |
| Llama 3 8B Instruct | 4-bit | ~0.5 s | 50–70 | 5.5 GB |
| Llama 3 8B Instruct | fp16 | ~0.7 s | 30–45 | 16 GB |
| Mixtral 8x7B | 4-bit | ~1.0 s | 25–35 | 26 GB |
| Llama 3.1 70B | 4-bit | ~3 s | 6–9 | 42 GB |

Numbers vary by exact chip, prompt length, and macOS version, but they give you the right order of magnitude. Compared to PyTorch+MPS on the same model, MLX with `mlx-lm` is usually 10–30% faster on the steady-state token rate.

### Fine-tuning with LoRA

`mlx-lm` ships a LoRA fine-tuner that's good enough for instruction-tuning a 7B–13B base on a Mac:

```bash
mlx_lm.lora \
    --model mlx-community/Mistral-7B-v0.3 \
    --train --data ./my_data \
    --iters 1000 --batch-size 2 --lora-layers 16
```

For anything bigger than 13B, expect to use `--lora-layers 8` and gradient checkpointing, and budget a couple of hours.

> **Gotcha.** Quantized weights are not bit-exact across q-group-size choices. If you swap from group=64 to group=128, expect mild quality drift. Re-evaluate downstream metrics; don't just trust the perplexity number.

> **Quick check.** Why is 4-bit Llama-3-8B faster on MLX than fp16 even though it does the same matmul? *(Memory bandwidth. The matmul is bandwidth-bound; reading a 4-bit weight is 4x cheaper than reading an fp16 one, and the unpack into fp16 happens in registers.)*

sections.title: MLX vs PyTorch+MPS: A Decision Rubric
sections.content:
### Pick MLX if...

- Your primary workload is **inference of open-weight LLMs** on Apple Silicon. `mlx-lm` is the clear winner today.
- You want **the largest possible model on a Mac** and need every byte of unified memory. MLX's lower overhead helps.
- You enjoy **functional / JAX-style programming** (`mx.grad`, `mx.vmap`, `mx.compile`) and want it on Apple hardware.
- You're **training a small-to-medium custom model** and don't need the Hugging Face ecosystem. MLX has a tighter loop and is often a hair faster.
- You're **prototyping research code** that will only ever run on Macs.

### Pick PyTorch+MPS if...

- You depend on **Hugging Face Transformers / Datasets / Accelerate / PEFT**. PyTorch is what these were built for.
- Your code needs to run on both **Mac (development) and Linux+CUDA (production)**. PyTorch is the only realistic portable answer.
- You want **rich tooling** — TensorBoard hooks, Lightning, PyTorch Profiler, `torch.compile`, FSDP. MLX is simpler but bare.
- You're using **niche PyTorch ops or third-party CUDA extensions** that MLX has no equivalent for.
- You want **mature distributed training**. MLX does not have an answer here yet.

### Use both

A reasonable hybrid pattern that I've found works well:

- **Prototype and iterate** in PyTorch with the MPS backend, reusing existing dataset / training-loop code.
- **Export the final weights** to MLX via `mlx_lm.convert` or a manual `safetensors` round-trip when you want the fastest local inference.
- **Serve** with `mlx-lm` (or a thin FastAPI wrapper around `mlx_lm.generate`) for chat-style local tools.

### Common porting tasks, side by side

```python
# PyTorch
import torch, torch.nn as nn
class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self): super().__init__(); self.fc = nn.Linear(8, 4)
    def forward(self, x): return torch.relu(self.fc(x))

model = MLP().to('mps')
opt = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), 1e-3)

for x, y in loader:
    x, y = x.to('mps'), y.to('mps')
    loss = ((model(x) - y) ** 2).mean()
    opt.zero_grad(); loss.backward(); opt.step()
```

```python
# MLX
import mlx.core as mx, mlx.nn as nn, mlx.optimizers as optim
class MLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self): super().__init__(); self.fc = nn.Linear(8, 4)
    def __call__(self, x): return nn.relu(self.fc(x))

model = MLP()
opt = optim.AdamW(learning_rate=1e-3)
loss_fn = lambda model, x, y: ((model(x) - y) ** 2).mean()
lvg = nn.value_and_grad(model, loss_fn)

for x, y in loader:
    loss, grads = lvg(model, x, y)
    opt.update(model, grads)
    mx.eval(model.parameters(), opt.state, loss)
```

The shapes line up. The differences — `__call__` vs `forward`, functional grads instead of `loss.backward()`, explicit `mx.eval` — are syntactic, not architectural. You'll be productive in the second style within an hour.
sections.code:
# Self-contained MLX example: train a tiny MLP on synthetic regression data.
import mlx.core as mx
import mlx.nn as nn
import mlx.optimizers as optim

mx.random.seed(0)

class TinyMLP(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_dim=4, hidden=32, out_dim=1):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(in_dim, hidden)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(hidden, out_dim)
    def __call__(self, x):
        return self.fc2(nn.relu(self.fc1(x)))

model = TinyMLP()
opt   = optim.AdamW(learning_rate=3e-3)

# Synthetic regression: y = sum of inputs + small noise
N = 1024
X = mx.random.normal((N, 4))
Y = X.sum(axis=1, keepdims=True) + 0.1 * mx.random.normal((N, 1))

def loss_fn(model, x, y):
    return ((model(x) - y) ** 2).mean()

lvg = nn.value_and_grad(model, loss_fn)

for step in range(200):
    # Mini-batch
    idx = mx.random.randint(0, N, (64,))
    xb = X[idx]; yb = Y[idx]
    loss, grads = lvg(model, xb, yb)
    opt.update(model, grads)
    mx.eval(model.parameters(), opt.state, loss)
    if step % 20 == 0:
        print(f"step {step:3d}  loss = {loss.item():.4f}")

print("\nfinal weight shapes:")
for name, p in nn.tree_flatten(model.parameters()):
    print(f"  {name}: {p.shape}")

references.title: MLX Community models on Hugging Face

quiz.question: In MLX, when does `c = a @ b` actually run on the GPU?
options: Immediately, like PyTorch eager mode
When you call `mx.eval(c)`, `print(c)`, `c.item()`, or convert to NumPy
Only after you call `mx.compile`
Only when memory pressure forces it
quiz.explanation: MLX is lazy: arithmetic builds a graph but does not execute it. Computation runs at materialization points — `mx.eval`, `print`, `.item()`, NumPy interop, etc. This lets MLX fuse across many statements before launching kernels.

quiz.question: Why does MLX have no `.to(device)` API?
options: Apple silicon has no GPU
It's planned for a future release
Unified memory means CPU and GPU read the same physical RAM, so there's no copy to perform
Because MLX always runs on CPU only
quiz.explanation: On M-series chips, CPU and GPU share one memory pool. There is literally no transfer to issue — switching the executing device is a scheduling decision, not a copy. MLX exposes this via `mx.set_default_device(...)` instead of a `.to('mps')` move.

quiz.question: What's the right place to call `mx.eval` in a training loop?
options: After every individual arithmetic operation
Never; MLX evaluates automatically when needed
Once per step on the loss, parameters, and optimizer state — at the boundary between iterations
Only when saving a checkpoint
quiz.explanation: Calling eval per op throws away most of the lazy benefit (no fusion). Never calling it lets graphs grow without bound and breaks the optimizer state between iterations. The idiomatic place is at the end of the step on `(loss, model.parameters(), optimizer.state)`.

quiz.question: Why is 4-bit Llama-3-8B usually faster than fp16 in mlx-lm even though both compute the same matmul?
options: 4-bit ops use the ANE; fp16 doesn't
Generation is bandwidth-bound, and 4-bit weights fetch 4x less memory; the dequantize-to-fp16 happens in registers
MLX skips half the layers in 4-bit mode
It isn't faster; that's a documentation error
quiz.explanation: Decoder inference at batch size 1 is dominated by reading weights from RAM. Cutting weight memory traffic 4x (fp16 -> 4-bit) cuts time roughly proportionally. The fp16 dequantize is fused into the matmul kernel and effectively free.

quiz.question: Which of these is unambiguously a reason to pick PyTorch+MPS over MLX, today?
options: You need the fastest local inference of a 4-bit Llama 3
You want the smallest possible memory footprint
You depend on Hugging Face Transformers, Datasets, or PEFT and on cross-platform CUDA portability
You want JAX-style `vmap` and `grad` transforms
quiz.explanation: MLX wins (or ties) on local LLM inference, memory footprint, and JAX-style transforms. PyTorch is the right answer when you depend on its ecosystem (Transformers, etc.) or need the same code to run on Linux+CUDA in production.

quiz.question: What does `mx.compile` do?
options: Compiles the entire MLX library from source
Traces a function once per input-shape signature, fuses ops, and caches the compiled kernel
Translates MLX code to MSL
Quantizes weights to 4 bits
quiz.explanation: `mx.compile` is MLX's analogue of `torch.compile` / `jax.jit`. It traces a Python function, hands the resulting graph to the MLX compiler, and caches the result keyed on shapes/dtypes.

quiz.question: In MLX, the forward pass of an `nn.Module` is implemented as:
options: A method named `forward`
A method named `__call__`
A free function passed to `nn.bind`
Whatever method has the `@nn.forward` decorator
quiz.explanation: MLX modules are plain callables. You define `__call__` and use `model(x)`. There's no `forward` method (a frequent porting tripwire from PyTorch).

exercise.title: Run a 4-bit LLM with mlx-lm and measure throughput
exercise.description:
Use `mlx-lm` to load a 4-bit quantized Llama 3 (or Mistral) instruct model from the `mlx-community` Hugging Face org and generate text from a prompt. Measure tokens/second over a 200-token completion and print the result.

**Requirements:**
- Install `mlx` and `mlx-lm` (`pip install mlx mlx-lm`).
- Use `mlx_lm.load` to load `mlx-community/Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct-4bit` (smaller, fits even 16 GB Macs). Fall back to a smaller model if RAM is tight.
- Build a chat prompt with `tokenizer.apply_chat_template`.
- Call `mlx_lm.generate` with `max_tokens=200`.
- Time the full call with `time.perf_counter()` and print `tokens/second`.
- Print the generated text.

**Hints:**
- `verbose=True` in `generate(...)` prints tokens-per-second when finished — handy for sanity-checking your timing.
- The model takes a few seconds to load the first time (downloads from HF Hub); subsequent runs hit the local cache.
- If you only have 16 GB RAM, prefer the 3B model. If you have 32+ GB, you can use `mlx-community/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct-4bit`.
- Wrap the timer around the `generate` call only, not the `load` call.
exercise.starterCode:
import time

# TODO: from mlx_lm import load, generate

MODEL_ID = "mlx-community/Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct-4bit"

# TODO: load model and tokenizer

exercise.solution:
import time
from mlx_lm import load, generate

# Pick a model that fits your RAM. 3B-4bit is ~2.5 GB and works on any M-series Mac.
MODEL_ID = "mlx-community/Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct-4bit"

model, tokenizer = load(MODEL_ID)

user_msg = "In one paragraph, explain why bfloat16 is preferred over float16 for training."
prompt = tokenizer.apply_chat_template(
    [{"role": "user", "content": user_msg}],
    tokenize=False,
    add_generation_prompt=True,
)

# Warm-up generate (compiles kernels, primes caches). The first call always pays a cost.
_ = generate(model, tokenizer, prompt=prompt, max_tokens=8)

MAX_NEW = 200
t0 = time.perf_counter()
text = generate(model, tokenizer, prompt=prompt, max_tokens=MAX_NEW)
elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0

# Estimate generated token count by re-tokenizing.
full_ids   = tokenizer.encode(text)
prompt_ids = tokenizer.encode(prompt)
gen_tokens = max(1, len(full_ids) - len(prompt_ids))

print(f"\n--- generated text ---\n{text[len(prompt):]}")
print(f"\n--- timing ---")
print(f"generated tokens: {gen_tokens}")
print(f"elapsed:          {elapsed:.2f} s")
print(f"throughput:       {gen_tokens / elapsed:.1f} tok/s")
